title: The Age of Innocence
author: Edith Wharton
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "The Age of Innocence" book by Edith Wharton?
Options:
A. Archer returned his visitor's worried look. "Are you not cognizant, Monsieur--is it conceivable you are unaware--that the family is beginning to doubt whether they should recommend the Countess decline her husband's latest offers?" "The offers you conveyed to her?" "The offers I relayed." Archer almost proclaimed that whatever he was privy to or not was no business of M. Riviere's. However, something in M. Riviere's humble yet staunch gaze made Archer reconsider this reaction. Instead, he met the young man's question with one of his own. "What is your aim in speaking to me about this?" He did not have to wait for the response.
B. Archer returned his guest's worried look. "Are you not aware, Monsieur--do you really not know--that the family is starting to wonder if they should recommend the Countess reject her husband's latest offers?" "The offers you conveyed?" "The offers I conveyed." Archer almost cried out that whatever he did or didn't know was none of M. Riviere's business. But something in the humble yet brave persistence of M. Riviere's gaze made Archer reconsider this reaction. Instead he answered the young man's question with one of his own. "Why are you telling me about this?" He did not have to wait at all for the response.
C. Archer gazed back at his anxious visitor. "Don't you realize, Monsieur--could it be you are unaware--that the family is questioning whether they should counsel the Countess to turn down her husband's recent proposals?" "The proposals you brought to her?" "Yes, the proposals I delivered." Archer nearly blurted out that whatever knowledge he had or lacked was no concern of M. Riviere's. But something in M. Riviere's humble yet determined stare made Archer change his mind. He answered the young man's inquiry with another question of his own. "What is your purpose in discussing this with me?" He did not have to linger for the reply.
D. Archer looked up and met his visitor's anxious gaze. "Don't you know, Monsieur--is it possible you don't know--that the family begin to doubt if they have the right to advise the Countess to refuse her husband's last proposals?" "The proposals you brought?" "The proposals I brought." It was on Archer's lips to exclaim that whatever he knew or did not know was no concern of M. Riviere's; but something in the humble and yet courageous tenacity of M. Riviere's gaze made him reject this conclusion, and he met the young man's question with another. "What is your object in speaking to me of this?" He had not to wait a moment for the answer.
Answer:
